name: Arévalo and Camino de Santiago del Sureste Loop
name_original: Ruinas del Convento de San Bernardo desde Arévalo — circular
description: Explore the moderate 27.3-mile Arévalo and Camino de Santiago del Sureste Loop, combining historic pilgrimage paths with local mountain biki
region_intro: Mountain biking around Arévalo offers routes through the flat, open terrain of the Castilian countryside, characterized by its diverse landscapes. The town is situated at the confluence of the Adaja and Arevalillo rivers, providing natural features that shape some of the trails. Riders can explore routes that connect with neighboring villages and pass by historic Mudejar architecture, including the Arévalo Castle. Viewpoints along the trails offer panoramic views of the old town and the Adaja River. Best mountain bike trails around Arévalo The most popular mountain bike trail is Circular Route of Arévalo and Mad, a 36.4 miles (58.5 km) trail that takes 4 hours 34 minutes to complete. This moderate route allows cyclists to explore the Castilian countryside. Another top favourite among local mountain bikers is Arévalo and Camino de Santiago del Sureste Loop, a moderate 27.3 miles (43.9 km) path. This route offers a journey through the region's historical and natural landscapes. Local mountain bikers also love the Arévalo Castle – Arevalillo Riverbank loop from Arévalo, a 22.8 miles (36.7 km) trail leading through riverbank areas and past the historic Arévalo Castle, often completed in about 2 hours 49 minutes. Mountain biking around Arévalo is defined by the flat Castilian countryside, the Adaja and Arevalillo rivers, and views of historic Mudejar architecture. The network includes easy, moderate, and difficult routes, catering to various skill levels. The routes in Arévalo are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from a few reviews. More than 100 mountain bikers have used komoot to explore Arévalo's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many mountain bike trails are available around Arévalo?
A: There are over 20 mountain bike trails available around Arévalo, catering to various skill levels from easy to difficult.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on mountain bike trails in Arévalo?
A: The mountain bike trails in Arévalo primarily traverse the flat, open terrain of the Castilian countryside. You'll find routes along the Adaja and Arevalillo rivers, offering scenic views and diverse landscapes. Some trails may also pass by historic Mudejar architecture.

Q: Are there mountain bike routes suitable for beginners in Arévalo?
A: Yes, Arévalo offers several easy mountain bike routes. There are 3 trails specifically categorized as easy, perfect for those new to mountain biking or looking for a relaxed ride.

Q: Are there challenging mountain bike trails for experienced riders in Arévalo?
A: Yes, for experienced riders seeking a challenge, there are 5 difficult mountain bike trails around Arévalo. These routes may include longer distances, more elevation gain, or sections that require pushing your bike.

Q: What do other mountain bikers say about the trails in Arévalo?
A: The mountain bike routes in Arévalo are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars. Over 100 mountain bikers have used komoot to explore the varied terrain, often praising the scenic countryside and historical points of interest.

Q: Are there any circular mountain bike routes in Arévalo?
A: Yes, many of the mountain bike routes around Arévalo are circular. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1186598151/arevalo-and-camino-de-santiago-del-sureste-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Arévalo and Camino de Santiago del Sureste Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 43.9 km path that offers a journey through the region's historical and natural landscapes.

Q: What historical landmarks or natural features can I see along the mountain bike trails?
A: Many trails offer views of Arévalo's rich historical and natural heritage. You can pass by the historic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5454716" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Arévalo Castle&lt;/a&gt;, explore the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5856915" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Plaza de la Villa&lt;/a&gt; with its Mudejar architecture, or ride along the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5768284" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Arevalillo Riverbank&lt;/a&gt;. Viewpoints along the routes provide panoramic views of the old town and the Adaja River.

Q: Are there any mountain bike routes that pass by the Arévalo Castle?
A: Yes, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38150671" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Arévalo Castle – Arevalillo Riverbank loop from Arévalo&lt;/a&gt; is a 36.7 km trail that leads through riverbank areas and directly past the historic Arévalo Castle, offering a blend of nature and history.

Q: What is a good moderate mountain bike route to explore the Castilian countryside?
A: The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1186568859/circular-route-of-arevalo-and-mad" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Circular Route of Arévalo and Mad&lt;/a&gt; is an excellent choice. This moderate 58.5 km trail takes approximately 4 hours 34 minutes to complete and allows cyclists to fully immerse themselves in the Castilian countryside.

Q: Are there mountain bike trails that connect Arévalo with other villages?
A: Yes, several routes connect Arévalo with neighboring villages. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20141787" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tolocirio – Montejo de Arevalo loop from Arévalo&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 60.1 km route that takes you through the wider region, linking different settlements.

Q: What is the longest mountain bike route available in Arévalo?
A: One of the longer routes is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24540195" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puente de Medina de Arévalo – Arévalo Castle loop from Arévalo&lt;/a&gt;. This difficult trail spans 66.2 km and involves over 300 meters of elevation gain, offering a substantial ride for those looking for distance.
tips: Here you will find the ruins of the Convent of San Bernardo. Most of the buildings are destroyed, but some walls still remain. There are several trails that lead here and most are good for mountain biking.
This is a nice little single track that runs alongside the Arevalillo River.

Plaza de la Villa is one of the most beautiful squares in Spain. Its architecture transports us to the Middle Ages, when it was the epicenter of social and economic life in Arévalo.
It is surrounded by buildings with arches, built with stone, wood and brick. The floor is cobbled, adding to its charm.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this mountain bike loop?
faqs.answer: The Arévalo and Camino de Santiago del Sureste Loop is designed to be completed in approximately 3 hours and 26 minutes of riding time. However, this duration can vary based on your fitness level, the number of stops you make, and the specific conditions of the trail sections you choose to combine.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this mountain bike route?
faqs.answer: The route combines sections of the Camino de Santiago del Sureste with local mountain biking trails around Arévalo. You can expect a diverse terrain featuring tracks with dirt and gravel, alongside meadows. Some sections may be relatively flat, while others involve unpaved paths that can become muddy when wet. Local loops around Arévalo often include a mix of paved surfaces and unpaved trails.

faqs.question: What can I see along the Arévalo and Camino de Santiago del Sureste Loop?
faqs.answer: Along the route, you'll encounter several points of interest. These include the historic Ruins of the Convent of San Bernardo, the scenic Arevalillo Riverside Trail, and the cultural landmarks of Plaza de la Villa and Church of San Martín in Arévalo. The route also offers diverse scenery, from Castilian meadows to views of the historic Arévalo Castle and surrounding countryside.

faqs.question: Is this mountain bike route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: This loop is rated as 'moderate' in difficulty. While some sections may be comfortable, others, particularly unpaved or hilly segments, can be challenging and may require good physical resistance. It's not a flat route, and some hills might even require pushing your bike. Riders should be prepared for diverse terrain and varying difficulty levels, making it more suitable for those with some mountain biking experience.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to mountain bike this route?
faqs.answer: The best time to mountain bike this route is generally during spring and autumn. These seasons offer more moderate temperatures and less chance of extreme heat or cold. During wet periods, dirt paths can become muddy, so checking weather conditions before your ride is advisable. The Camino del Sureste is known for being less crowded than other pilgrimage routes, offering a more tranquil experience.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start the loop in Arévalo?
faqs.answer: While specific parking areas for this self-curated loop are not designated, Arévalo, as a historical town and a stop on the Camino de Santiago, typically offers various public parking options within or near the town center. It's recommended to look for parking close to the historic areas or the starting point you choose within Arévalo.

faqs.question: Is the Arévalo and Camino de Santiago del Sureste Loop dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: Generally, dogs are allowed on many public trails in Spain, especially on rural paths like those found on the Camino de Santiago. However, it's always best to keep your dog on a leash, especially when passing through populated areas, near livestock, or on shared paths. Be sure to carry water for your dog, as sources may be limited on some sections.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay entrance fees for this mountain bike route?
faqs.answer: No, you do not need any special permits or have to pay entrance fees to ride the Arévalo and Camino de Santiago del Sureste Loop. The route primarily uses public paths, including sections of the Camino de Santiago and local trails around Arévalo, which are freely accessible.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route incorporates a significant section of the Camino de Santiago del Sureste (22% of the route). It also includes smaller segments of other official trails such as Paseo fluvial (4%), Camino de Pajares de Adaja (4%), Camino del Bohodoncillo (3%), and Colada del Camino de Pedro Rodriguez (3%), among others.

faqs.question: Are there places to find food, drinks, or accommodation along the route?
faqs.answer: Arévalo is a historical town and a stop on the Camino de Santiago, so it offers various amenities including cafes, pubs, and accommodation options. As you venture out of Arévalo onto the more rural sections, especially along the Camino del Sureste, services may become less frequent. It's advisable to carry sufficient water and snacks, especially for longer stretches between towns.

faqs.question: What type of bike is best suited for this loop?
faqs.answer: Given the varied terrain, which includes dirt, gravel, and potentially muddy unpaved paths, a mountain bike (MTB) is highly recommended for this loop. The route is not suitable for road bikes. A capable mountain bike with good suspension and appropriate tires will provide the best experience and handle the diverse conditions effectively.

waypoints.name: Ruins of the Convent of San Bernardo
Plaza de la Villa and Church of San Martín, Arévalo
